core_story.problem: Introduction Video generation [ 7, 13, 18, 20, 24, 45, 59] has made rapid progress in recent years.
core_story.limitation: However, in order to produce effective lens language, such as multi- camera shooting, dolly zoom, and object motion editing in videos, video creators often need to have more precise 4D control over the video content.
core_story.insight: Fortunately, we observe that the videos rendered from the Gaussian ﬁeld although contain artifacts, still provide strong cues for video generation.
core_story.proposed_method: Speciﬁcally, we propose a novel framework that constructs dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds with dense 3D point tracking and renders the Gaussian ﬁeld for all video frames.
core_story.experimental_evidence: Our D3D-PT outperforms SpatialTracker , the state-of-the-art sparse 3D point tracking method, in accuracy and accelerates the inference speed This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
core_story.summary: Introduction Video generation [ 7, 13, 18, 20, 24, 45, 59] has made rapid progress in recent years. However, in order to produce effective lens language, such as multi- camera shooting, dolly zoom, and object motion editing in videos, video creators often need to have more precise 4D control over the video content. Fortunately, we observe that the videos rendered from the Gaussian ﬁeld although contain artifacts, still provide strong cues for video generation. Speciﬁcally, we propose a novel framework that constructs dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds with dense 3D point tracking and renders the Gaussian ﬁeld for all video frames. Our D3D-PT outperforms SpatialTracker , the state-of-the-art sparse 3D point tracking method, in accuracy and accelerates the inference speed This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.

introduction_structure.evidence_text:
1. Introduction
Video generation [
7, 13, 18, 20, 24, 45, 59] has made
rapid progress in recent years. Since the demonstration of
Sora [
7], the video generated by Diffusion Transformers
(DiT) is approaching cinematic quality by scaling up the
parameters and the number of training videos. However,
in order to produce effective lens language, such as multi-
camera shooting, dolly zoom, and object motion editing in
videos, video creators often need to have more precise 4D
control over the video content. For example, multi-camera
shooting requires the presentation of the same content from
different perspectives and dolly zoom requires adjusting the
intrinsic and extrinsic parameters of the camera at the same
time. Y et, current video generation methods are unable to
support such 4D video control.
Generative Camera Dolly (GCD) [
49] demonstrates the
possibility of utilizing the generative model for multi-
camera shooting. After preparing multi-camera shooting
videos as training data, it switches videos captured by dif-
ferent camera trajectories and the same dynamic contents
as input condition video and output supervision. However,
capturing a large amount of synchronized multi-traject

limitation_expression: However, in order to produce effective lens language, such as multi- camera shooting, dolly zoom, and object motion editing in videos, video creators often need to have more precise 4D control over the video content.

However, capturing a large amount of synchronized multi-trajectory videos in open-door real scenes is too expensive, e.g., car driving videos, so GCD chooses to collect training data in a simulator, which exhibits poor generalization perfor- mance to the real world and prohibits it from being trained on web videos.
However, the optimization process is highly time-consuming, requiring at least one hour for a 70-frame video, which makes the preparation of training data impractical.
Fortunately, we observe that the videos rendered from the Gaussian ﬁeld although contain artifacts, still provide strong cues for video generation.
insight_expression: Fortunately, we observe that the videos rendered from the Gaussian ﬁeld although contain artifacts, still provide strong cues for video generation.
method_expression: Speciﬁcally, we propose a novel framework that constructs dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds with dense 3D point tracking and renders the Gaussian ﬁeld for all video frames.

The main con- tributions of this paper are summarized as follows: • We propose a novel framework that advances video gen- eration with dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds through efﬁcient dense 3D point tracking. • We propose an efﬁcient dense 3D point tracking (D3D- PT) method that outperforms SpatialTracker in accuracy, and accelerates dense 3D point tracking by two orders of magnitude.
D3D-PT facilitates the training of video gen- eration from a rendered dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁeld. • We propose GS-DiT that learns to generate videos follow- 21718 ing the guidance of rendered videos.

Inspired by classical Monocular Dynamic Video Synthe- sis (MDVS) methods [ 14, 15, 54] that render novel view videos by obtaining a 4D representation, we propose a novel framework that learns 4D video control with the help of dy- namic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds.
experiment_expression: In this section, we ﬁrst introduce the efﬁ- cient dense 3D point tracking (D3D-PT), which is the cor- nerstone of the dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds in training, and then demonstrate the Gaussian ﬁeld construction.

notable_sentences: D3D-PT facilitates the training of video gen- eration from a rendered dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁeld. • We propose GS-DiT that learns to generate videos follow- 21718 ing the guidance of rendered videos.
Fortunately, we observe that the videos rendered from the Gaussian ﬁeld although contain artifacts, still provide strong cues for video generation.

However, in order to produce effective lens language, such as multi- camera shooting, dolly zoom, and object motion editing in videos, video creators often need to have more precise 4D control over the video content.
However, the optimization process is highly time-consuming, requiring at least one hour for a 70-frame video, which makes the preparation of training data impractical.
Speciﬁcally, we propose a novel framework that constructs dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds with dense 3D point tracking and renders the Gaussian ﬁeld for all video frames.
A pretrained Video DiT is ﬁnetuned with the rendered re- sults as input to generate the original input video from the rendered video, dubbed as GS-DiT.

Abstract 4D video control is essential in video generation as it en- ables the use of sophisticated lens techniques, such as multi- camera shooting and dolly zoom, which are currently un- supported by existing methods.

After preparing multi-camera shooting videos as training data, it switches videos captured by dif- ferent camera trajectories and the same dynamic contents as input condition video and output supervision.

Besides the camera pose control, we can also adjust the camera in- trinsics and edit the Gaussian ﬁeld to support more 4D video editing effects Our proposed framework does not require multi-view videos for training.

By bringing dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds to video generation, we have endowed our GS-DiT with the 4D controllability from Gaussian ﬁelds, such as camera intrinsic and extrinsic editing and object motion editing.
Compared to GCD, GS-DiT presents a strong generalization capacity and can be consistently improved when the base DiT model is scaled up.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
4D video control is essential in video generation as it en-
ables the use of sophisticated lens techniques, such as multi-
camera shooting and dolly zoom, which are currently un-
supported by existing methods. Training a video Diffusion
Transformer (DiT) directly to control 4D content requires
expensive multi-view videos. Inspired by Monocular Dy-
namic novel View Synthesis (MDVS) that optimizes a 4D
representation and renders videos according to different 4D
elements, such as camera pose and object motion editing,
we bring dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds to video generation.
Speciﬁcally, we propose a novel framework that constructs
dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds with dense 3D point tracking
and renders the Gaussian ﬁeld for all video frames. Then we
ﬁnetune a pretrained DiT to generate videos following the
guidance of the rendered video, dubbed as GS-DiT. To boost
the training of the GS-DiT, we also propose an efﬁcient
Dense 3D Point Tracking (D3D-PT) method for the dynamic
3D Gaussian ﬁeld construction. Our D3D-PT outperforms
SpatialTracker , the state-of-the-art sparse 3D point tracking
method, in accuracy and accelerates the inference speed
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
21717

by two orders of magnitude. During the inference stage,
GS-DiT can generate videos with the same dynamic content
while adhering to different camera parameters, addressing
a signiﬁcant limitation of current video generation mod-
els. GS-DiT demonstrates strong generalization capabili-
ties and extends the 4D controllability of Gaussian splatting
to video generation beyond just camera poses. It supports
advanced cinematic effects through the manipulation of the
Gaussian ﬁeld and camera intrinsics, making it a power-
ful tool for creative video production. Demos are available
at
https://wkbian.github.io/Projects/GS-
DiT/.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Video generation [
7, 13, 18, 20, 24, 45, 59] has made
rapid progress in recent years. Since the demonstration of
Sora [
7], the video generated by Diffusion Transformers
(DiT) is approaching cinematic quality by scaling up the
parameters and the number of training videos. However,
in order to produce effective lens language, such as multi-
camera shooting, dolly zoom, and object motion editing in
videos, video creators often need to have more precise 4D
control over the video content. For example, multi-camera
shooting requires the presentation of the same content from
different perspectives and dolly zoom requires adjusting the
intrinsic and extrinsic parameters of the camera at the same
time. Y et, current video generation methods are unable to
support such 4D video control.
Generative Camera Dolly (GCD) [
49] demonstrates the
possibility of utilizing the generative model for multi-
camera shooting. After preparing multi-camera shooting
videos as training data, it switches videos captured by dif-
ferent camera trajectories and the same dynamic contents
as input condition video and output supervision. However,
capturing a large amount of synchronized multi-trajectory
videos in open-door real scenes is too expensive, e.g., car
driving videos, so GCD chooses to collect training data
in a simulator, which exhibits poor generalization perfor-
mance to the real world and prohibits it from being trained
on web videos. This raises the question: can we directly
learn multi-camera shooting video generation from normal
monocular videos? One straightforward solution is to op-
timize a 4D Gaussian ﬁeld [
2, 54, 56] for each monocular
video and then reprogram the camera trajectories to render
novel view videos for training. However, the optimization
process is highly time-consuming, requiring at least one
hour for a 70-frame video, which makes the preparation of
training data impractical. Furthermore, due to the ill-posed
nature of 4D video optimization, the rendered videos suf-
fer from signiﬁcant artifacts. Fine-tuning video generation
models with these videos will inevitably degrade the video
quality.
Fortunately, we observe that the videos rendered from
the Gaussian ﬁeld although contain artifacts, still provide
strong cues for video generation. Drawing inspiration from
recent works on classical monocular dynamic video synthe-
sis (MDVS) [
15], we build the dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds
and render the novel view videos to guide the video gener-
ation. Speciﬁcally, we ﬁrst propose an efﬁcient dense 3D
point-tracking (D3D-PT) method to facilitate the dynamic
3D Gaussian ﬁeld construction. Given an input video, we
select a reference frame and estimate its dense 3D point tra-
jectories across the subsequent frames. The dynamic 3D
Gaussian ﬁeld is then directly constructed from 3D point
trajectories without any optimization. The Gaussian prim-
itives [
30] of the ﬁeld have constant color values derived
from the pixel values, replacing the view-dependent spheri-
cal harmonic representation. Other parameters of the Gaus-
sian primitives, such as opacity, are all held constant. We
then generate the training data by rendering the Gaussian
ﬁelds using the original camera poses from the input video.
A pretrained Video DiT is ﬁnetuned with the rendered re-
sults as input to generate the original input video from the
rendered video, dubbed as GS-DiT. Since it is difﬁcult to
annotate the ground truth of 3D point tracking in the real
world, point trackers are trained on synthetic data, and the
depth distribution in the real world may be quite different
from that in synthetic data. Our D3D-PT is loosely coupled
to the depth distribution so that can be ﬂexibly adapted to
different scenarios. In the inference stage, given a monoc-
ular dynamic video, we also build a dynamic 3D Gaussian
ﬁeld and render it according to the newly assigned cam-
era parameters. Then, our ﬁnetuned DiT generates the ﬁnal
video following the guidance of the rendered video. Besides
the camera pose control, we can also adjust the camera in-
trinsics and edit the Gaussian ﬁeld to support more 4D video
editing effects
Our proposed framework does not require multi-view
videos for training. GS-DiT can therefore be trained on
any monocular dynamic videos and learn various motion
dynamics. Compared to GCD, GS-DiT presents a strong
generalization capacity and can be consistently improved
when the base DiT model is scaled up. GS-DiT can also
support more 4D controllability than GCD, such as camera
intrinsic control and object motion editing. The main con-
tributions of this paper are summarized as follows:
• We propose a novel framework that advances video gen-
eration with dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds through efﬁcient
dense 3D point tracking.
• We propose an efﬁcient dense 3D point tracking (D3D-
PT) method that outperforms SpatialTracker in accuracy,
and accelerates dense 3D point tracking by two orders of
magnitude. D3D-PT facilitates the training of video gen-
eration from a rendered dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁeld.
• We propose GS-DiT that learns to generate videos follow-
21718

ing the guidance of rendered videos. GS-DiT can gener-
ate multi-camera shooting videos and provides 4D video
control, e.g., camera intrinsic, and object motion editing.
raw_sections.method:
3. Method
Achieving precise control over 4D content in video gener-
ation is essential for integrating this technology into ﬁlm
production. To better accommodate video generation in cin-
ematic studios, we tackle the 4D video control problem.
Inspired by classical Monocular Dynamic Video Synthe-
sis (MDVS) methods [
14, 15, 54] that render novel view
videos by obtaining a 4D representation, we propose a novel
framework that learns 4D video control with the help of dy-
namic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds. In contrast to GCD [
49] which
learns multi-camera shooting video generation from multi-
view synchronized videos, our proposed framework directly
learns from conventional monocular videos and can be nat-
urally scaled up along with the base video Diffusion Trans-
formers (DiT). In this section, we ﬁrst introduce the efﬁ-
cient dense 3D point tracking (D3D-PT), which is the cor-
nerstone of the dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds in training, and
then demonstrate the Gaussian ﬁeld construction. Finally,
we elaborate on how to ﬁne-tune a pre-trained DiT to gen-
erate videos with 4D control.
raw_sections.experiments:
4. Experiments
We ﬁrst evaluate our proposed dense 3D point tracking
(D3D-PT) and then evaluate our 4D video control frame-
work GS-DiT. Finally, we show the controllability of GS-
DiT with dolly zoom and object motion editing. We strongly
suggest readers refer to the supplemented video .
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
We have proposed a framework for 4D video control. By
bringing dynamic 3D Gaussian ﬁelds to video generation,
we have endowed our GS-DiT with the 4D controllability
from Gaussian ﬁelds, such as camera intrinsic and extrinsic
editing and object motion editing. Limitation. Our dy-
namic 3D representation is not optimized. We look forward
to generating explicit 4D Gaussian ﬁelds with our GS-DiT
in the future.
21724
